summary: I have witnessed Jacksonville grow tremendously, especially the south side along the St. Johns River over the past ten years. As a former member of the Jacksonville City Commission, I advocated for improvements like voting machines and better city administration. I also worked on annexing nearby suburbs, improving roads, parks, and public services to make Jacksonville a better place for its residents.

The St. Johns River has always been a central feature of Jacksonville's landscape, especially the south side where beautiful homes and estates line the riverfront. Over the last decade, I've seen the area transform from old farms and plantations into modern city lots with magnificent homes. The river and its surroundings have been key to the area's development and appeal.

The Florida East Coast Railway played a significant role in Jacksonville's commerce, with many yellow-painted frame buildings housing its employees. The railway's network of tracks was used for switching operations, and its presence influenced the development and character of the areas around Jacksonville. It was part of the industrial backdrop during the time I was involved in city affairs.

As Park Commissioner, I pushed for Duval County to purchase land for parks, including a 27-acre tract adjoining a swamp that became a fine city park. I also envisioned beautifying the county with a 100-foot-wide scenic drive around its beaches, lined with evergreens and native plants. My goal was to enhance the county's natural beauty and create enjoyable spaces for residents and visitors alike.

I tried for eight years through two governors' administrations to improve the Florida Legislature's efficiency, including proposing three sessions a year to avoid rushed work at the end. I also advocated for a state police training school and better legislative scheduling to save taxpayer money. Unfortunately, some sessions, like the 1939 one, accomplished little despite costing half a million dollars.

Florida has been the setting for much of my public service, from advocating for better police training to improving city and county infrastructure. I worked to protect residents by proposing undercover agents to track dangerous criminals statewide. My efforts aimed to enhance safety, governance, and the quality of life across Florida communities.

While my work has mostly focused on Florida and Jacksonville, the United States context is always present, especially regarding federal cooperation on law enforcement. I supported collaboration with the federal Board of Investigation to locate dangerous criminals headed for Florida. This partnership was vital to maintaining law and order in our state and nation.

I proposed establishing a state police training school attached to the penal institution at Hullos, where trainees could observe criminals firsthand. This school would have provided intensive six-year training for young men aspiring to be efficient law enforcement officers. Unfortunately, despite my efforts, this plan was never realized, but I believed it would have greatly benefited Florida's police system.

Ambler, Steketee and Bassett was a firm operating a bank in Jacksonville during the early 1880s, located where the Consolidated Building now stands. Arthur D. Bassett, a New Yorker and lawyer, was associated with this firm and held a responsible position. He later went into business as a broker and handled legal affairs, including those of A.V. Rest.

The Consolidated Building in Jacksonville stands where the bank operated by Ambler, Steketee and Bassett once was in the early 1880s. This location has historical significance as a financial center in the city's development. I recall that the bank started in 1877 near where Turner's store was located before moving to this site.

I never knew anyone named Henry Morton Stanley in Jacksonville, and I believe the famous explorer was English and not connected to our city. There may have been a reporter with a similar name here, but it was not the same person who wrote 'In Darkest Africa' in the 1880s. So, any association with Jacksonville is mistaken in my view.

Arthur D. Bassett was a New York man involved with the firm Ambler, Steketee and Bassett in Jacksonville during the early 1880s. He was quite smart and held a responsible position with the firm, which operated a bank. Later, he became a business broker and lawyer, handling important affairs such as those of A.V. Rest.

W. B. Barnett was a notable figure in Jacksonville's history, having lived here many years ago. The bank that eventually operated near the Consolidated Building was started in 1877, and while I knew of old man E. B. Barnett and his sons, I do not recall specific details about W. B. Barnett beyond his connection to the city’s early financial institutions.

During my time as a member of the Jacksonville City Commission, I was the first to advocate for voting machines to combat illegal and corrupt voting practices, successfully bringing them to the city. I also pushed for efficiency in city government by proposing the appointment of a man to study and improve operations. My tenure was marked by efforts to modernize and improve Jacksonville’s governance.

I advocated for improved roadways connecting Jacksonville to the beaches, including a well-paved road and a 200-foot-wide circle roadbed from South Jacksonville to the beaches. I also wanted to establish a ferry from St. George to Mayport to create a scenic circle drive to the oceanfront. These efforts aimed to enhance access and enjoyment of Jacksonville Beach and nearby oceanfront areas.
